ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Roomali Roti. The broader flatbread class FOODON_03315050 is used as the closest defensible anchor. A specific term for Roomali Roti is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E (Unleavened bread) is the closest defensible category. No Roomali Roti-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically for Roomali Roti was identified in the published SNOMED CT release. Omitted rather than fabricated.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard roomali roti piece weighs approximately 60 g, representing a single large, thin handkerchief-sized flatbread as typically served in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Roomali Roti / Refined Wheat Flour Flatbread
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for maida (refined wheat flour) chapati / roti. Roomali roti is made from maida with minimal fat and water; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN maida roti entry (cooked, per 100 g edible portion). The very thin cooking method results in slightly lower moisture retention compared to standard chapati; values are consistent with the ICMR-NIN maida-based flatbread profile. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, chapati or roti, plain, commercially prepared' (FDC ID 174263) was used as a cross-reference to validate macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — refined wheat flour flatbread / chapati analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Roomali Roti was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Roomali roti is made from refined wheat flour (maida) with no leavening, cooked as a very thin flatbread. The closest analogous food is white wheat flour chapati / roti (GI approximately 62, glucose reference), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entries for unleavened refined wheat flatbreads. The thin cooking method does not substantially alter starch gelatinisation relative to standard maida roti. GI = 62 (medium) is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 60.5 × 0.60) / 100 = 22.4 g per 60 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and refined carbohydrate load
hypertension.guidance: Roomali roti contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with high-sodium curries or chutneys to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and high refined carbohydrate content from maida
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roomali roti has a medium GI of approximately 62 and a glycemic load of ~22 per piece, driven by refined wheat flour (maida) with minimal fibre; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict to one piece per meal, pair with high-fibre vegetables or legume-based curries to blunt postprandial glucose response, and prefer whole-wheat alternatives where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roomali roti is low in saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a relatively heart-friendly bread option; however, its refined flour base provides little dietary fibre, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour whole-grain flatbreads and monitor total carbohydrate intake to support lipid management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roomali Roti (also spelled Rumali Roti) is a traditional North Indian and Mughal-origin unleavened flatbread made from refined wheat flour (maida). The name derives from the Urdu/Hindi word 'rumal' meaning handkerchief, reflecting the bread's characteristic thinness and large diameter. The dough is prepared with maida, water, salt, and a small amount of oil or ghee, then hand-stretched or tossed until paper-thin before being cooked on the convex outer surface of an inverted kadai (wok) over an open flame. This distinctive cooking method produces a soft, pliable, slightly chewy flatbread with a delicate texture and mild flavour. Roomali roti is served folded like a handkerchief and is a popular accompaniment to tandoori dishes, kebabs, and rich Mughlai curries. In Singapore, it is offered at Indian restaurants, particularly those specialising in North Indian and Mughlai cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 297 kcal, 8 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and 60.5 g carbohydrate, with modest dietary fibre (2.1 g) due to the use of refined flour. Its medium glycemic index (~62) means it raises blood glucose more rapidly than whole-wheat alternatives. Clinically, it is suitable in moderate portions for most adults but should be consumed cautiously by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing weight, given its refined carbohydrate content and low fibre density.

confidence_reason: No exact Roomali Roti entry was found in ICMR-NIN published tables; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN maida roti / chapati profile and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for plain roti. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for refined wheat flour unleavened flatbreads. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Roomali Roti-specific nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) maida roti entry and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174263, chapati/roti plain). No ICMR-NIN entry specifically labelled 'Roomali Roti' was identified; adaptation is justified by identical ingredient composition (maida, water, salt, minimal fat). 2. GI = 62 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour unleavened flatbread data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct Roomali Roti GI measurement was found in the published literature. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN maida roti source entry. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN maida roti source entry. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN maida roti source entry. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN maida roti source entry. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN maida roti source entry. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived fat in standard vegan preparation). 10. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — refined wheat flatbread contains no vitamin C). 11. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). 12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived fat or fortification in traditional preparation). 13. Chinese names (鲁马利薄饼 / 魯馬利薄餅) are transliterations used for retrieval purposes; no established Chinese culinary name exists for this dish. 14. Serving size of 60 g is based on typical restaurant portion for one piece of roomali roti as observed in Indian restaurant contexts in Singapore. 15. Price range SGD 4.43–8.90 preserved from field survey source for contextual reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source.

Documented all -1 sentinel values and all zero values in notes. Confirmed all hard gates pass and score 8/8.
